stats.changes: Although it's too early to make conclusions the incentives seem to have increased the daily activity.
stats.lessons:
- During the first week, we experimented with various Sybil prevention mechanisms. These included owning a Farcaster account, having a Gitcoin Passport Score of 20+ and owning at least 10 Layer3 CUBEs. 

- We noticed that unreliable Farcaster APIs often resulted in verification fails. As a result, we plan to utilize the Gitcoin passport and Layer3 CUBEs as the preferred mechanism.

actuals.summary:
- Over the last seven days we launched the Arbitrum Hub campaign including 10 Quests on Layer3. 
- Arbitrum Hub Quests have received ~95,000 participations
- 40,000 ARB have been allocated to individual Quest contracts
- 18,000 ARB have been claimed by users
actuals.disclosures: We received the first disbursement a week late, on June 10th, which caused a delay in starting the distribution. To make up for lost time, we plan to accelerate the campaign in the upcoming weeks and align with the KPIs and timeline originally outline in the application.

planned.changes: N/A - This period marks the launch of 10 Quests as initially planned via the grant.
planned.expectations:
We are incentivizing actions within 10 Arbitrum ecosystem apps, with users earning between 0.2 to 0.4 depending on the complexity of actions. 
We expect all Quests to be fully claimed and to achieve individual KPIs within 1 month of launch.

stats.changes:
- Canceled Clipper Quest due to a malicious Discord Invite in their documentation
- Launched Matcha Quest instead of Aevo
stats.lessons: We deployed 25 Quests, allocating the total 100,000 ARB as planned for the first month. However, some Quests were deployed in the last few days, so the full distribution hasn’t occurred yet. As we close Phase 1 and move to Phase 2, we’ll ensure to launch the Quests earlier to ensure full distribution according to the plan.

actuals.summary:
Our anti-sybil mechanisms, combining CUBEs and Gitcoin Passport, aim to ensure the highest quality of users receive the Grant. According to our metrics dashboard, we are close to achieving most of our planned metrics for Month 1. Considering the 1-week delay mentioned in the first report, we have been able to catch up on launches and will be able to meet planned targets for Phase 2.
Total Quest launched: 25 Quests
Total ARB allocated: 100,000 ARB
Total ARB distributed: 70,000 ARB
Unique Users: 44,000
actuals.disclosures:
We launched a Quest featuring Clipper. However, due to a malicious Discord Invite in their documentation, we have removed the Quest to ensure the safety of our users and won’t include Clipper in upcoming Quests.
We changed the initial planned Quest for Aevo to Matcha

planned.changes:
- To accelerate progress and meet our KPIs, we increased the supply of Quests. 
- This adjustment aligns us with our Phase 1/ Month 1 plan: 100,000 ARB, 20+ unique dApps, and 25 Quests.
planned.expectations:
- Quest are being well-received by our user base, and we are on track with our KPIs. 
- At the end of this cycle, we will conclude Phase 1: Test, review all launched Quests, and improve distribution based on lessons learned.

stats.changes: We removed Umami and Savvy from the initial planned Quests. At the end of Phase 1, we allocated 65,000 ARB as rewards for 12,500 users actively engaged in the campaign.
stats.lessons: The gaming sector presents a challenge in finding compatible apps that support the Layer3 traffic. Campaign prizes tend to motivate users to continue Questing and reward those actively engaged.

actuals.summary: We are observing strong user adoption on the Arbitrum Quests. Additionally, we have allocated an extra 65,000 ARB from our internal budget to provide additional incentives and rewards for power-users participating in the campaign.
actuals.disclosures:
After reviewing over 30 gaming/NFT apps, selecting a few capable of handling Layer3 traffic is challenging due to the high volume of user activity.

We’ve launched Quests with 0xAstra, Bridgeworld, Treasure, and OpenSea, alongside multiple DeFi-centered apps. We are also planning a coordinated launch with Cosmik Battle.

We had to alter our plans for Umami and Savvy, as QA revealed they couldn’t handle the Layer3 traffic.

planned.expectations:
We are starting Phase 2, where we will broaden the quest offering within the Arbitrum ecosystem and iterate on incentive models based on Phase 1.

We aim to launch 15 quests bi-weekly, focusing more on expanding into Gaming and NFTs.

stats.changes: No major changes were made to our campaign strategy. We continued with our planned launches and increased the supply of Quests in the first week of each bi-weekly period. This ensures users have sufficient time to claim their rewards, enhancing overall engagement and reward distribution.
stats.lessons:
We are finalizing the second stage, Iteration, and moving towards Retention. In this phase, our focus will be on targeting users who completed previous Quests and rewarding those with active liquidity on Arbitrum, particularly from a DeFi perspective.

From a gaming perspective, we’ve launched Quests that require users to engage for more than 7 days. This has resulted in good user retention, with many players continuing to participate and requesting more Quests in the gaming sector. This approach is proving effective in maintaining user interest and engagement.

actuals.summary:
We launched all our planned 25 Quests ahead on the first week of the period, to ensure users had ample time to participate and claim their ARB rewards.

This early launch has been positively received by the community, resulting in a cohort of extremely active and engaged users on the Arbitrum network through these Quests. The proactive approach has enhanced user experience and engagement.
actuals.disclosures: No specific disclosures are needed at this time.

planned.changes: We are progressing according to plan, with 15 new Quests for this bi-weekly period.
planned.expectations:
We’ll close this period with 55 Quests launched, aiming to have 60K ARB fully distributed, so far featuring 53 unique Arbitrum dapps in our campaign.

This period is more DeFi-oriented, while we analyze the results of previous gaming initiatives and re-schedule for the upcoming period.

stats.changes: Focusing on DeFi apps and ensuring user retention on Arbitrum.
stats.lessons: High-quality projects with a good UX are preferred by users, and we’re doubling down to ensure a great experience while creating initiatives to drive retention.

actuals.summary: We’ve allocated rewards to projects in accordance with the planned grant.

planned.changes:
We are gearing toward the final phase of our distribution strategy, which is focused on retention. In this stage, we aim to ensure that the most aligned and engaged users receive the ARB rewards. 

This approach is designed to reward and retain users who have shown consistent participation and alignment with our community’s goals.
planned.expectations:
In the final phase of our distribution strategy, we aim to attract and engage the highest value users to the Arbitrum Network. 

Our focus will be on exploring and expanding across all previously shared categories, including gaming, DeFi, bridges, and NFTs. 

This phase is designed to ensure that we not only retain our most dedicated users but also continue to grow and diversify our community by tapping into these key areas.

stats.changes: We’ve launched the Quests ahead of schedule to give users ample time to complete actions and claim rewards.

actuals.summary: We’ve allocated all 400,000 ARB from the LTIP to 100 Quests, with 4,000 ARB per Quest across a wide range of apps, as planned in the proposal.
actuals.disclosures: We’ve fully allocated resources to 100 Quests.

planned.expectations: Focusing on retention and high-quality apps, optimizing to hit all KPIs.

stats.changes: We’ve allocated all 400,000 ARB from the LTIP to 100 Quests, with 4,000 ARB per Quest across a wide range of apps, as planned in the proposal. The rewards are currently in the claiming phase by users.
